key_insight: In molten FLiNaK, corrosion in NiCr alloys is controlled first by surface crystallography and then by grain-boundary character, with fluorine-driven chromium loss and vacancy-mediated motion causing grain boundary migration away from the dealloyed zone.
engineering_relevance: Low-energy special grain boundaries such as coherent Σ3(111) may improve corrosion resistance, making grain-boundary engineering a promising design strategy for Ni-Cr alloys in molten-salt environments.
method: Compared four grain boundaries: Σ3(111), Σ11(113), Σ5(012), and Σ5(013)
evidence_signal: The study uses atomistic simulation evidence across four representative grain boundaries and surface orientations to link corrosion behavior, localization, and interfacial migration.
watch_item: Results are from reactive molecular dynamics, so transfer to real alloy service conditions may require validation against temperature, composition, and time-scale effects.
paper_title: Atomistic mechanism of corrosion-induced grain boundary migration in NiCr alloys in molten FLiNaK
